questions.question: Which of the following best describes how Notion AI respects a user’s existing workspace permissions?

options.text: It can view all pages in the workspace regardless of the user’s access rights.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Notion AI does NOT bypass permissions; it can only use content the user already has access to.

options.text: It only processes data that the requesting user is allowed to see, honoring all permission settings.
options.explanation: Correct – The article states that Notion AI honors existing permissions and cannot see information the user lacks access to.

options.text: It ignores permissions but encrypts the data before processing.
options.explanation: Wrong – Encryption is a separate security measure; permission handling is explicit and not ignored.

options.text: It asks the workspace owner for temporary elevated access before each query.
options.explanation: Incorrect – No such temporary elevation mechanism is described in the article.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of generating embeddings for Notion pages?

options.text: To store the original page content in an encrypted format.
options.explanation: Embeddings are not encrypted copies of the original text; they are numerical vectors representing meaning.

options.text: To create a semantic, numerical representation that enables fast similarity searches.
options.explanation: Correct – The article explains embeddings capture meaning in a multidimensional space for efficient vector search.

options.text: To train Notion’s own language model on user data.
options.explanation: Wrong – Notion does not use customer data to train its models; embeddings are used only for retrieval.

options.text: To replace the original document with a shorter summary.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Embeddings are not summaries; they are abstract numerical representations.

questions.question: Which compliance certifications does Notion AI fall under according to the article?

options.text: SOC 2 Type 2 and ISO 27001
options.explanation: Correct – The article states Notion AI is included in the scope of Notion’s SOC 2 Type 2 report and ISO 27001 certification.

options.text: PCI DSS and GDPR
options.explanation: Wrong – These standards are not mentioned as applicable to Notion AI in the article.

options.text: FedRAMP and CMMC
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article does not reference these certifications for Notion AI.

options.text: HIPAA only
options.explanation: Partially true – Notion AI enables HIPAA compliance, but the question asks for certifications, which are SOC 2 Type 2 and ISO 27001.

questions.question: How does Notion AI ensure that embeddings are protected with the same level of security as other Customer Data?

options.text: By storing them in a publicly accessible cloud bucket with read‑only permissions.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Publicly accessible storage would not meet security commitments.

options.text: By applying the commitments outlined in the Master Service Agreement (MSA) and Data Processing Agreement (DPA) to the embeddings.
options.explanation: Correct – The article says embeddings receive the same security and privacy considerations as Customer Data per the MSA and DPA.

options.text: By deleting embeddings immediately after each query is answered.
options.explanation: Wrong – Embeddings are retained for a period (up to 60 days after page deletion) to support future queries.

options.text: By encrypting them with a proprietary algorithm that only Notion developers know.
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article does not mention a proprietary algorithm; it references standard security commitments.

questions.question: What steps must a customer take to receive notifications about new Subprocessors used by Notion AI?

options.text: Sign up on the Notion website’s notification center.
options.explanation: The article does not mention a website notification center; it specifies an email process.

options.text: Email [email protected] with the subject “Subscribe to New Subprocessors.”
options.explanation: Correct – This is the exact method described for subscribing to Subprocessor notifications.

options.text: Enable the “Subprocessor Alerts” toggle in Workspace Settings → Notion AI.
options.explanation: No such toggle is mentioned in the article.

options.text: Request a dedicated account manager to forward Subprocessor updates.
options.explanation: While possible, this is not the prescribed method in the article.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the data retention policy for embeddings when a Notion page is deleted?

options.text: Embeddings are permanently retained to improve future AI responses.
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article states embeddings are deleted within 60 days of page or workspace deletion.

options.text: Embeddings are deleted within 60 days after the page or workspace is deleted.
options.explanation: Correct – This matches the retention timeline described in the article.

options.text: Embeddings are immediately removed, but a backup is kept for 90 days.
options.explanation: The article does not mention immediate removal or a 90‑day backup.

options.text: Embeddings are retained indefinitely for compliance auditing.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Retention is limited; indefinite storage is not described.

questions.question: How does Notion AI handle Customer Data when it is transmitted to third‑party Subprocessors?

options.text: It sends the data in plain text to reduce latency.
options.explanation: Wrong – The article specifies encryption in transit using TLS 1.2 or greater.

options.text: It encrypts the data in‑transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
options.explanation: Correct – This is the encryption protocol mentioned for data sent to Subprocessors.

options.text: It only transmits metadata, never the actual content.
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article does not say that only metadata is transmitted.

options.text: It relies on the Subprocessor’s own encryption without additional safeguards.
options.explanation: Wrong – Notion adds its own TLS encryption in transit, regardless of Subprocessor measures.

questions.question: Which statement accurately reflects Notion’s policy on using Customer Data to train its AI models?

options.text: Customer Data is routinely used to improve Notion’s proprietary models unless the user opts out.
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article says Notion does NOT use Customer Data to train models by default.

options.text: By default, Notion and its Subprocessors are contractually prohibited from using Customer Data to train any models.
options.explanation: Correct – The article specifies contractual agreements prevent use of Customer Data for training.

options.text: Only Enterprise customers’ data is exempt from training, while other plans’ data may be used.
options.explanation: Wrong – The policy applies universally, not just to Enterprise customers.

options.text: Notion uses Customer Data for training only after a 90‑day retention period.
options.explanation: Incorrect – No such delayed training policy is described.

questions.question: What are the two configurable settings available to workspace owners for controlling Notion AI’s web search behavior?

options.text: Enable web search for the workspace and Require confirmation for web requests.
options.explanation: Correct – These are the two settings described in the article.

options.text: Limit web search to trusted domains and Disable external image analysis.
options.explanation: Incorrect – Those specific controls are not mentioned in the article.

options.text: Activate AI‑generated summaries only and Turn off PDF analysis.
options.explanation: Wrong – These are features, not the configurable web‑search settings.

options.text: Set a maximum number of web results and Enable automatic citation generation.
options.explanation: Incorrect – These options are not listed as settings in the article.

questions.question: How can Enterprise plan customers receive alerts when sensitive data is used in an AI prompt or generated output?

options.text: By enabling the built‑in Notion AI DLP module in Settings → Notion AI.
options.explanation: Incorrect – The article states DLP alerts are provided via third‑party integration partners, not a built‑in module.

options.text: By configuring third‑party DLP integrations available on the Enterprise plan.
options.explanation: Correct – The article explains Enterprise customers can trigger DLP alerts through third‑party integrations.

options.text: By requesting a custom webhook from Notion’s support team for each AI request.
options.explanation: Wrong – No such custom webhook mechanism is described.

options.text: By turning on “Sensitive Content Logging” in the Workspace Security panel.
options.explanation: Incorrect – That specific toggle is not mentioned in the article.
